personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Draco is defined by a consistent pattern of drawling arrogance, class-based contempt, and performative cruelty — but also by a fragility that surfaces under pressure. He is verbally sharp, uses insults as social weapons (Mudblood, blood traitor, poverty-shaming the Weasleys), and relies on his father's name as a shield. He brags loudly, sneers constantly, and uses Crabbe and Goyle as an audience. Crucially, the books also show his limits: he panics in the forest (screaming and fleeing from the cloaked figure), fakes injury to milk sympathy and threaten Hagrid, cries in the bathroom in HBP, and is visibly terrified on the Astronomy Tower. His voice is drawling, cold, and performative — but the books show the performance cracking.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the drawling sneer, the class contempt, the Mudblood slur, and the reliance on his father's name. Tom Felton's delivery captures the lazy insolence well. The films include key moments: the Borgin and Burkes scene, the Mudblood incident, the Quidditch rivalry, the ferret scene, the Astronomy Tower confrontation, the Malfoy Manor scenes, and the Room of Requirement. The bathroom breakdown is present. The films also add a moment not in the books — Draco tossing Harry his wand in the final battle — which is a significant invention.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose much of Draco's petty, sustained verbal cruelty across years of schooling. The books show him taunting Harry about his dead parents at Christmas, mocking Neville repeatedly, doing a cruel impression of Colin Creevey, writing a caricature of Harry being struck by lightning, and singing 'Weasley is our King' while marking Malfoy's malicious glee. The films compress this into fewer, more dramatic confrontations. The sustained pattern of low-level cruelty that defines his personality in years 1-4 is largely absent. His cowardice in the forest (screaming and fleeing) is present but the books show more instances of his bravado collapsing. The invented wand-toss in the final battle adds a redemptive beat not in the books.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — arrogant, contemptuous, fragile under pressure — is preserved. The drawling voice, the class snobbery, the Mudblood slur, the father-name-dropping are all present. The penalty is for the loss of the sustained petty cruelty pattern across years 1-4 (the Christmas taunting, the Quidditch match mockery, the Weasley-is-our-King singing, the dementor impressions) which in the books builds a texture of relentless low-level malice. The invented wand-toss is a minor addition that softens him slightly without basis. These are meaningful but not transformative losses.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Draco serves multiple narrative functions across the series: antagonist and social foil to Harry in years 1-4, unwitting informant (Polyjuice scene), escalating threat (Buckbeak case, Lucius's machinations), and finally the central dramatic pivot of HBP — the student given an impossible mission who cannot complete it. He drives plot in books 1-3 through his provocations and his father's interference. In HBP he is the primary dramatic engine: his mission to repair the Vanishing Cabinet, his attempts on Dumbledore's life, his confrontation on the Astronomy Tower, and his ultimate failure to kill are the spine of the book's tension. He also functions as a moral test case — Dumbledore's argument that his soul is not yet lost.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his role as antagonist in years 1-3, his function in the Polyjuice scene, and his central role in HBP. The Astronomy Tower confrontation is faithfully adapted. His role in Deathly Hallows — the Malfoy Manor scenes, the Room of Requirement, the final battle — is present. The films do show him as the pivot of HBP's tension.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several agency losses are notable. In the books, Draco's sustained campaign in HBP — the Vanishing Cabinet repair, the Rosmerta Imperius, the poisoned mead, the necklace — is shown as his active, desperate work over months. The films compress this and show less of his agency in executing these plans. The Polyjuice scene in CoS is faithfully adapted. However, the books show Draco actively tipping off Filch about the midnight duel (book 1), actively using Rita Skeeter (book 4), and actively lobbying Umbridge as part of the Inquisitorial Squad (book 5) — the last of which is present in the films but compressed. The invented wand-toss in the final battle gives him an agency beat he does not have in the books, where he is simply absent from the final confrontation.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: His core narrative role is preserved — antagonist, HBP mission-holder, Astronomy Tower pivot. The penalty is for the compression of his active agency in executing the HBP plans (less shown of his months of work), the loss of some year 1-5 provocations that drive plot (the midnight duel tip-off, the Quidditch match sabotage attempts), and the invented final-battle wand-toss which transfers a redemptive agency beat from a non-existent book moment.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Draco's motivations are layered. In years 1-4, he is driven by a desire to prove his superiority, please his father, and maintain his social position. His contempt for Muggle-borns is genuine ideology absorbed from Lucius. In HBP, his motivations become acutely conflicted: he has been given a mission he cannot refuse (kill Dumbledore or Voldemort will kill him and his family), he is terrified, he is desperate to prove himself, and he is simultaneously proud of being chosen and horrified by what it requires. The Astronomy Tower scene makes explicit that he cannot bring himself to kill — his wand drops before the Death Eaters arrive. The bathroom scene shows him crying, saying 'I can't do it... he'll kill me.' His internal conflict between ideology, family loyalty, fear, and a residual moral limit is the emotional core of HBP.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core HBP motivation: the impossible mission, the fear of failure, the family threat. The bathroom scene is present. The Astronomy Tower confrontation preserves his inability to kill and his explicit statement 'I've got to kill you or he'll kill me.' The Malfoy Manor scenes show his reluctance to identify Harry. The films do show his conflict.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films lose the gradual revelation of his motivations across HBP — the months of visible deterioration, the overheard conversation with Snape at the Christmas party where he says 'I was chosen! This is my moment!' and 'I won't fail him,' the Apparition lesson where he argues with Crabbe about how long it's taking. These scenes build the texture of his desperation and pride. The films also lose the book's explicit framing that Voldemort chose Draco as punishment for Lucius's failure — a motivation that adds a layer of tragic injustice to his situation. Snape's conversation with Dumbledore about this (present in the film corpus) is retained, but Draco himself never learns or articulates this.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation — impossible mission, family threat, inability to kill — is preserved and clearly communicated. The penalty is for the loss of the gradual build of his desperation and pride across HBP, and the loss of the Lucius-punishment framing as a layer of his situation. These are simplifications rather than changes, so the score remains in the 'simplified but correct' range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Draco's arc across the series moves from arrogant bully (years 1-4) to increasingly desperate and compromised agent of Voldemort (HBP) to a figure whose moral limits are exposed and who, by the end of DH, is neither fully redeemed nor fully villainous. The arc's key turning points: the Buckbeak case (his father's power used maliciously), the Polyjuice scene (revealed as not the Heir), HBP (given an impossible mission, deteriorates visibly, cannot kill), DH (reluctance at Malfoy Manor, the Malfoy family's growing estrangement from Voldemort). The epilogue shows him as a father, nodding curtly to Harry — a cold peace, not a warm reconciliation. The arc is not one of redemption but of a character whose ideology and cruelty are shown to have limits, and whose family's survival becomes the overriding value.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve the major arc beats: the bully years, the HBP deterioration, the Astronomy Tower failure, the Malfoy Manor reluctance, the Room of Requirement rescue, the final battle wand-toss, the epilogue nod. The arc is present.
character_arc.difference: The films add the wand-toss in the final battle, which is a redemptive beat not in the books and slightly accelerates the arc toward reconciliation. The epilogue nod is present in both. The films lose the gradual year-by-year deterioration of his position — his strutting after Dumbledore's suspension in CoS, his gleeful cruelty in years 3-4, his visible decline in HBP (the grey skin, the dark circles, the skipping of Quidditch). The arc is compressed but the key turning points are present. The invented wand-toss is the most significant arc deviation — it gives Draco an active redemptive choice in the final battle that the books do not give him.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is recognizable and the key turning points are present. The penalty is for the invented wand-toss (an arc beat not in the books that moves him further toward active redemption) and the compression of the gradual deterioration. These are meaningful but the arc remains substantially faithful.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Draco's most important relationships: (1) Lucius — a relationship of admiration, dependence, and eventual humiliation as Lucius falls from favor; Draco's identity is built on his father's name and power. (2) Harry — sustained rivalry and mutual contempt, with Harry as the foil who always bests him. (3) Snape — genuine respect and favoritism, which in HBP becomes complicated as Draco resents Snape's interference and suspects him of wanting to steal his glory. (4) Narcissa — the relationship that most clearly shows his vulnerability; she fights for him, makes the Unbreakable Vow, and lies to Voldemort to save him. (5) Crabbe and Goyle — followers who in DH begin to defy him, marking his loss of authority.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Lucius relationship is present and the dynamic of Draco being subordinate to his father is shown. The Harry rivalry is present. The Snape relationship in HBP is shown in the corridor scene. The Narcissa relationship is shown in the Unbreakable Vow scene and Malfoy Manor. Crabbe and Goyle's defiance in the Room of Requirement is present.
key_relationships.difference: The Draco-Snape relationship in HBP is significantly compressed. In the books, Draco has been avoiding Snape all year, resents his interference, and in the Christmas party corridor scene explicitly accuses Snape of wanting to steal his glory — a complex dynamic of resentment toward a former mentor. The films show this scene but compress it. The Draco-Lucius dynamic loses the book's texture of Draco's pride in his father giving way to embarrassment and then pity as Lucius is humiliated. The Draco-Crabbe/Goyle relationship loses the book's detail of Draco using Polyjuice to disguise them as girls — a detail that shows his authority and their degradation.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are present and their basic dynamics are preserved. The penalty is for the compression of the Draco-Snape dynamic in HBP (a genuinely complex relationship that the films simplify) and the loss of some texture in the Draco-Lucius dynamic across the series.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The books give Draco considerable complexity: his cowardice is shown repeatedly (fleeing in the forest, faking injury, crying in the bathroom) alongside his genuine cruelty; his ideology is shown as absorbed rather than purely innate (his father's views, his upbringing); his relationship with Snape in HBP is genuinely ambivalent; his deterioration across HBP is shown in physical detail (grey skin, dark circles, skipping Quidditch); his inability to kill is framed by Dumbledore as evidence that his soul is not yet fully damaged; his family loyalty ultimately overrides his ideology. The Polyjuice scene reveals he is not the Heir of Slytherin — a deflation of his own mythology. The books also show his petty sustained cruelty across years 1-4 in granular detail.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the bathroom breakdown, the Astronomy Tower failure, the Malfoy Manor reluctance, and the physical deterioration in HBP. The Polyjuice deflation is present. The family loyalty theme is present.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The films lose: (1) the sustained petty cruelty pattern across years 1-4 that builds his texture as a bully (the Christmas taunting, the Quidditch mockery, the dementor impressions, the Weasley-is-our-King singing); (2) the granular detail of his HBP deterioration (skipping Quidditch, the months of visible decline); (3) the complexity of his Snape relationship in HBP; (4) the Polyjuice scene detail of him using Crabbe and Goyle as disguised girls; (5) the book's framing of his situation as punishment for Lucius's failure, which adds tragic dimension. The invented wand-toss adds a redemptive nuance not in the books. The overall effect is a Draco who is recognizable but somewhat flattened — his cruelty is shown in highlights rather than as a sustained pattern, and his complexity in HBP is present but compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The most important nuances — the bathroom breakdown, the Astronomy Tower failure, the Malfoy Manor reluctance, the family loyalty — are preserved. The penalty is for the loss of the sustained cruelty texture across years 1-4, the compression of HBP complexity, and the invented redemptive beat. These losses affect the audience's understanding of how deeply cruel and how genuinely conflicted he is.

lost_or_transferred_material: Sustained petty cruelty pattern across years 1-4 (Christmas taunting about Harry's dead parents, dementor impressions after Quidditch loss, Weasley-is-our-King singing, dementor imitation in Great Hall)
Draco tipping off Filch about the midnight duel in year 1
Draco using Polyjuice-disguised Crabbe and Goyle as girls to stand guard outside the Room of Requirement
Draco's months of visible physical deterioration in HBP (grey skin, dark circles, skipping Quidditch matches)
The framing that Voldemort chose Draco as punishment for Lucius's failure — a layer of tragic injustice
The complexity of Draco's resentment toward Snape in HBP (accusing him of wanting to steal his glory, avoiding him all year)
Draco's active role in the Inquisitorial Squad in OotP (docking points, cornering Harry, reporting to Umbridge) — present but compressed
Draco tossing Harry his wand in the final battle (invented for the films, not in the books — a transferred/invented redemptive agency beat)
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (above 75) considered: The invented wand-toss transfers an agency/arc beat to Draco that does not exist in the books. However, this is an addition rather than a transfer from another character, and the overall characterization remains substantially faithful. Cap 3 does not strictly apply as no book action was given to another character — rather a new action was invented. Score of 71 is below 75 regardless.
Cap 1 (above 85) not triggered: sufficient complexity and nuance is preserved in the films.
comparative.key_observations: Draco Malfoy is one of the more faithfully adapted characters in the film series, with Tom Felton's performance capturing the core personality well. The films preserve the most dramatically important material: the Polyjuice scene, the Mudblood incident, the Buckbeak case, the HBP mission and deterioration, the Astronomy Tower confrontation, the Malfoy Manor scenes, and the Room of Requirement. The primary losses are the sustained texture of petty cruelty across years 1-4 (which the books build through dozens of small scenes), the compression of his HBP complexity (particularly his resentment of Snape), and the loss of the Lucius-punishment framing. The most significant deviation is the invented wand-toss in the final battle, which gives Draco an active redemptive choice not present in the books and slightly misrepresents his arc's endpoint. The result is a Draco who is recognizable and substantially faithful but somewhat simplified — his cruelty shown in highlights rather than as a sustained pattern, and his HBP conflict present but compressed.
